items.title: Vector transmission of T. cruzi
items.contentHtml:
Trypanosoma cruzi vector transmission

  • Main vector: triatomines (Triatoma infestans, Rhodnius prolixus, Panstrongylus megistus).
  • Mechanism: the insect defecates during the bite; The feces contain metacyclic trypomastigotes that penetrate through the wound or mucous membranes.
  • Reservoirs: dogs, cats, rodents and armadillos (domestic and jungle cycle).
  • Vector transmission is the most frequent route in endemic areas; Chagas disease is a zoonosis.

items.title: Chronic chagasic heart disease
items.contentHtml:
Chronic Chagasic heart disease

  • Pathophysiology: chronic myocarditis with fibrosis, damage to the autonomic nervous system and microvasculopathy.
  • Findings: complete right bundle branch block (BCRD) + left anterior hemiblock (HBAI) on ECG.
  • Complications: dilated cardiomyopathy, apical aneurysm of the left ventricle (typical), ventricular arrhythmias, sudden death.
  • Kuschnir classification: G0 (normal ECG, no cardiomegaly), G1 (abnormal ECG, absent cardiomegaly), G2 (mild/moderate cardiomegaly), G3 (heart failure).

items.title: Romagna sign
items.contentHtml:
Romagna sign

  • Romagna sign: unilateral bipalpebral edema, painless, violaceous, with conjunctivitis and dacryoadenitis.
  • It occurs due to inoculation of the parasite into the ocular mucosa (vector route).
  • It is pathognomonic of the acute phase in children, but is not always present (only ~50% of cases). acute).
  • DDx: orbital cellulitis, allergy, trauma; The absence of high fever and the painless evolution point to acute Chagas disease.

items.title: Acute phase with myocarditis
items.contentHtml:
Acute febrile phase with myocarditis

  • Prolonged fever, hepatomegaly, splenomegaly, lymphadenopathy, and in children acute myocarditis with heart failure.
  • ECG: sinus tachycardia, prolongation of the PR interval, negative T waves.
  • In the acute phase, trypomastigotes are detected in peripheral blood. (thick smear/smear).
  • Acute mortality ~2-5% due to myocarditis or meningoencephalitis.

items.title: Megaesophagus and megacolon
items.contentHtml:
Megaesophagus and Chagasic megacolon

  • Due to denervation of the myenteric plexus (Auerbach) by T. cruzi.
  • Megaesophagus: dysphagia, odynophagia, regurgitation, sialorrhea; Rezende radiological classification (I to IV).
  • Megacolon: chronic constipation, abdominal pain, fecaloma; risk of sigmoid volvulus.
  • DDx: megaesophagus due to idiopathic achalasia (more common in non-endemics); megacolon due to Hirschsprung's disease (absence of lymph nodes in biopsy).

items.foco: Diagnosis with thick smear or smear
items.title: Thick drop and smear in acute
items.contentHtml:
Diagnosis with thick smear or smear

  • Indicated in the acute phase (first 4-8 weeks) or reactivation.
  • Thick smear or smear of peripheral blood stained with Giemsa or Wright: identifies motile trypomastigotes.
  • High sensitivity in acute phase (>90%); in chronic phase it is low (<10%).
  • In chronic phase serology (IgG) is used.

items.title: IgG serology in chronic phase
items.contentHtml:
IgG serology in chronic phase

  • Method of choice in chronic phase: two positive serological tests (ELISA, IFI, HAI) with different antigens.
  • IgG anti-T. cruziis detectable throughout life; IgM only in acute.
  • Western blot or PCR as confirmation in discordant cases.
  • Trap: in immunosuppressed patients serology may be negative; use PCR.

items.title: Xenodiagnosis and PCR
items.contentHtml:
Xenodiagnosis and PCR in acute

  • Xenodiagnosis: classic parasitological method (feed triatomines free of infection with the patient's blood and examine their feces). Useful in chronic with low parasitemia.
  • PCR (polymerase chain reaction): high sensitivity in acute and reactivation; quantifies parasite load.
  • In chronic phase the PCR is qualitative, not always positive; Its negativity does not rule out infection.

items.title: Benznidazole
items.contentHtml:
Benznidazole treatment

  • First line: benznidazole 5 mg/kg/day PO every 12 hours for 60 days (adults); children: 5-10 mg/kg/day.
  • Absolute indications: acute phase, reactivation, congenital, children <12 years, recent chronic (<5 years).
  • Adverse effects: allergic dermatitis (30%), peripheral neuropathy, myelosuppression (discontinue if leukocytes <3000/mm³).
  • Contraindicated: pregnancy, liver/renal failure serious.

items.title: Nifurtimox
items.contentHtml:
Nifurtimox as an alternative

  • Nifurtimox 8-10 mg/kg/day PO every 6-8 hours for 60-90 days (adults); children: 15-20 mg/kg/day.
  • Alternative when there is intolerance or resistance to benznidazole.
  • Adverse effects: anorexia, weight loss, neurotoxicity (irritability, insomnia, polyneuropathy), gastrointestinal.
  • Do not use in pregnancy or liver failure.

items.title: Transfusion and congenital transmission
items.contentHtml:
Transfusion and congenital transmission

  • Transfusion transmission: prevented by mandatory serological screening in blood banks in endemic areas.
  • Congenital transmission: occurs in 1-10% of infected pregnant women; can cause abortion, prematurity, low weight, neonatal myocarditis.
  • Diagnosis in newborn: thick smear and PCR at birth; if negative, IgG serology at 9-12 months (after maternal antibodies disappear).
  • Treatment: benznidazole in newborns (5-10 mg/kg/day for 60 days).

items.title: Reactivation in HIV
items.contentHtml:
Chagas disease reactivation in HIV

  • Reactivation occurs when CD4 <200 cells/μL, typically with meningoencephalitis (space-occupying lesions on CT/MRI) or myocarditis.
  • Diagnosis: thick smear, PCR in blood and CSF; Trypomastigotes are observed in CSF.
  • Treatment: benznidazole 5 mg/kg/day for 60 days; in intolerance use nifurtimox.
  • Primary prophylaxis: not routinely indicated; ART is key to avoiding reactivation.
